The **English Democrats** are a [far-right](/wiki/Far-right_politics "Far-right politics"),[[5]](#cite_note-Far-right-5) [English nationalist](/wiki/English_nationalism "English nationalism") [political party](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of political parties in the United Kingdom") active in England. A [minor party](/wiki/Minor_party "Minor party"), it currently has no elected representatives at any level of government.

The English Democrats were established in 2002 by members of the Campaign for an English Parliament [pressure group](/wiki/Pressure_group "Pressure group"). Following growing political [devolution in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom"), which had seen the creation of the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), [Welsh Parliament](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd") and [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly"), the party's founders called for a separate [English Parliament](/wiki/Devolved_English_parliament "Devolved English parliament"). In the 2000s, it obtained a small number of local councillors. In 2009, the party's candidate, [Peter Davies](/wiki/Peter_Davies_(politician) "Peter Davies (politician)"), was elected Mayor of the [Metropolitan Borough of Doncaster](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Doncaster "Metropolitan Borough of Doncaster"), although he left the party in 2013 in protest at its admittance of former members of the [fascist](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party") (BNP). As well as attracting many ex-BNP members, who then constituted a sizeable percentage of the English Democrats' electoral candidates, in 2015 the political party [Veritas](/wiki/Veritas_(political_party) "Veritas (political party)") merged into it.

Ideologically committed to English nationalism, the party previously called for [England to become an independent state](/wiki/English_independence "English independence"), thus leaving the United Kingdom. Since 2016, it has instead called only for the creation of a devolved English Parliament within a federal UK. It has also called for a referendum on whether [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire "Monmouthshire"), a county presently recognised as being in Wales, should instead be classified as part of England. The party is [Eurosceptic](/wiki/Euroscepticism "Euroscepticism") and endorsed [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In 1998, in response to calls for the [devolution](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") of power to [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), [Robin Tilbrook](/wiki/Robin_Tilbrook "Robin Tilbrook") aimed at reforming the [English National Party](/wiki/English_National_Party "English National Party"), which had ceased operating in 1981. This project included members of the [Campaign for an English Parliament](/wiki/Devolved_English_parliament "Devolved English parliament"), a [pressure group](/wiki/Advocacy_group "Advocacy group") that lobbies for a devolved English Parliament. The party was relaunched as the "English Democrats" in September 2002, after merging with several other smaller political parties. In October 2004 the party merged with the Reform UK Party, which was a small splinter group from the [United Kingdom Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") (UKIP). The [New England Party](/wiki/New_England_Party "New England Party") merged with the English Democrats in February 2007.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The English Democrats were co-founders of the English Constitutional Convention,[[10]](#cite_note-10) now defunct.

In December 2004, it was rumoured that [Robert Kilroy-Silk](/wiki/Robert_Kilroy-Silk "Robert Kilroy-Silk"), the former UKIP [MEP](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament") had entered into negotiation to join the English Democrats.[[11]](#cite_note-11) However, Kilroy-Silk formed [Veritas](/wiki/Veritas_(political_party) "Veritas (political party)") instead.

In 2007, the columnist and conspiracy theorist [Vernon Coleman](/wiki/Vernon_Coleman "Vernon Coleman") announced he had joined the English Democrats.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The party's most significant electoral success came when Peter Davies (a former UKIP and Reform UK member), its candidate for Mayor of Doncaster, was [elected](/wiki/2009_Doncaster_Council_mayoral_election "2009 Doncaster Council mayoral election"). Having received 16,961 votes in the first round, 189 votes behind the [independent](/wiki/Independent_(politician) "Independent (politician)") Michael Maye, Davies was returned in the second count on transfers of second preference votes, with 25,344 votes to 24,990.[[13]](#cite_note-DoncasterMayor2009-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Mayoral_Election_2009_Results-14) However, Davies announced his resignation from the party on 5 February 2013 citing "a big influx of new members joining from the British National Party".[[15]](#cite_note-resignation-15) One of its councillors, Mick Glynn, resigned the following day after the party's chairman, Tilbrook, launched a personal attack on Davies, thus reducing its number of elected representatives to two.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The English Democrats lost their remaining councillors in the 2015 local elections. On 18 September 2015, [Veritas](/wiki/Veritas_(political_party) "Veritas (political party)") merged into the English Democrats.[[17]](#cite_note-Merger_with_the_English_Democrats-17) Of the party officers, only the party leader, Therese Muchewizc, joined the English Democrats filling the roles of Deputy Chairman and National Council Member, the remaining officers refusing to do so due to differences in policy beliefs.[[17]](#cite_note-Merger_with_the_English_Democrats-17)

The party claimed a total membership of 1,011 at the end of 2004, and 1,202 at the end of 2005.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In 2015, the party lost the vast majority of its general election minimum 5% of poll [deposits](/wiki/Deposit_(politics) "Deposit (politics)"), had a few councillors elected and gained some by defection and was regarded by some as a [fringe](/wiki/Political_fringe "Political fringe") party.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Parliamentary elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Parliamentary elections")]

At the [2004 Birmingham Hodge Hill by-election](/wiki/2004_Birmingham_Hodge_Hill_by-election "2004 Birmingham Hodge Hill by-election") the English Democrat candidate received 277 votes, or 1.4% of the votes cast.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The party's slogan for the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election") was "The English Democrats – Putting England First!" In total, the English Democrats fielded 25 candidates for the May 2005 general election,[[28]](#cite_note-28) including [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") South where the election was delayed until June due to the death of a candidate. The party withdrew its candidate in [North Norfolk](/wiki/North_Norfolk_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Norfolk (UK Parliament constituency)") and endorsed the Conservative Party candidate, [Iain Dale](/wiki/Iain_Dale "Iain Dale"), as he had "taken the issues of English discrimination seriously".[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[Garry Bushell](/wiki/Garry_Bushell "Garry Bushell"), the former *[Sun](/wiki/The_Sun_(United_Kingdom) "The Sun (United Kingdom)")* journalist and current *[Daily Star Sunday](/wiki/Daily_Star_(United_Kingdom) "Daily Star (United Kingdom)")* TV critic, became the most high-profile candidate for the English Democrats, standing in the [Greenwich and Woolwich](/wiki/Greenwich_and_Woolwich_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Greenwich and Woolwich (UK Parliament constituency)") constituency in [London](/wiki/London "London").[[30]](#cite_note-30) Bushell's 1,216 votes (3.4%) beat the [UKIP](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") candidate, Stan Gain, who secured 709 votes (2.0%);[[31]](#cite_note-31) this was the party's best result for the election though still a fifth-place performance.

In June 2005, Bushell also stood in [Staffordshire South](/wiki/South_Staffordshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "South Staffordshire (UK Parliament constituency)"), where he received 643 votes (2.5%) coming fifth out of eight candidates.[[32]](#cite_note-32) In 2011, Bushell announced that he would, in future, be supporting UKIP.

The English Democrats fielded Joanne Robinson as their candidate in the [by-election](/wiki/2008_Haltemprice_and_Howden_by-election "2008 Haltemprice and Howden by-election") forced by the resignation of former shadow home secretary [David Davis](/wiki/David_Davis_(British_politician) "David Davis (British politician)") from the House of Commons. Because of [the issues raised by David Davis](/wiki/David_Davis_by-election_campaign,_2008 "David Davis by-election campaign, 2008") in the by-election, many parties other than the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), such as [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"), [United Kingdom Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") (UKIP) and [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party") (BNP) chose not to stand. Joanne Robinson came third, with 1,714 votes (7.2%), 44 votes fewer than the Green candidate received in second place. Of the 26 candidates she was one of only three to win back her deposit. This result is both the highest place gained and the highest percentage of the votes won by any English Democrat candidate in a parliamentary election or parliamentary by-election.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/English_Democrats_771_DYE.JPG/250px-English_Democrats_771_DYE.JPG)](/wiki/File:English_Democrats_771_DYE.JPG)

[Campaign bus](/wiki/Campaign_bus "Campaign bus") during the 2010 general election

The English Democrats stood 107 candidates in the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"). 106 is the minimum number required to qualify for a Party Election Broadcast. The English Democrats received 64,826 votes, or 0.3% of the vote in England, and 0.2% of the vote in the United Kingdom. No candidates were elected[[34]](#cite_note-34) but the party saved one deposit in the [Doncaster North](/wiki/Doncaster_North_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Doncaster North (UK Parliament constituency)") constituency, where candidate Wayne Crawshaw picked up 5.2% of the vote.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In subsequent by-elections, the party contested [Oldham East and Saddleworth](/wiki/2011_Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth_by-election "2011 Oldham East and Saddleworth by-election") (where Stephen Morris polled 144 (0.4%)),[[36]](#cite_note-36) [Barnsley Central](/wiki/2011_Barnsley_Central_by-election "2011 Barnsley Central by-election") (Kevin Riddiough polled 544 (2.2%) votes),[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Feltham and Heston](/wiki/2011_Feltham_and_Heston_by-election "2011 Feltham and Heston by-election") (Roger Cooper polled 322 (1.4%)),[[38]](#cite_note-38) [Corby](/wiki/2012_Corby_by-election "2012 Corby by-election") (David Wickham polled 432 (1.2%) votes)[[39]](#cite_note-39) and [Rotherham](/wiki/2012_Rotherham_by-election "2012 Rotherham by-election") (David Wildgoose polled 703 (3.3%) votes).[[40]](#cite_note-40)

At the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"), the party contested 32 seats, securing a total of 6,531 votes (0.02%).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the [2016 Batley and Spen by-election](/wiki/2016_Batley_and_Spen_by-election "2016 Batley and Spen by-election"), the English Democrats received 4.8% of the votes, coming second to Labour's [Tracy Brabin](/wiki/Tracy_Brabin "Tracy Brabin"), when all of the other major parties did not stand out of respect for the murdered MP, [Jo Cox](/wiki/Jo_Cox "Jo Cox").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### European Parliament elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: European Parliament elections")]

#### 2004

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2004")]

The English Democrats stood candidates for the [2004 European Parliament election](/wiki/2004_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2004 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom") in five of the nine regions of England. The party's 2004 election canvassing leaflet featured the slogan, "Not left, not right, just English". Its candidates received 130,056 votes in total.

#### 2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2009")]

In June 2009, the English Democrats contested [elections to the European Parliament](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2009 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"). The party fielded a full slate of candidates across the nine [English European parliament constituencies](/wiki/Regions_of_England "Regions of England"). On 18 May 2009, the English Democrats broadcast their first national [Party Election Broadcast](/wiki/Party_political_broadcast "Party political broadcast").[[41]](#cite_note-41) They came seventh in the election in England (ninth in the UK as a whole) with 279,801 votes or 1.8%, a rise from the 0.7% they received at the previous elections in 2004. The English Democrats do not stand in Northern Ireland, Scotland or Wales, and their vote across England in 2009 was 2.1%, an increase of 1.3% yet still did not keep their deposits except in Yorkshire and the Humber where they gained 2.6% of the vote. None of the English Democrats candidates were elected; the English Democrats were the highest-polling party across the UK not to have an MEP elected.

#### 2014

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2014")]

The English Democrats began their [2014 EU election campaign](/wiki/2014_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2014 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom") in September 2013 with an extensive social media campaign. On 30 April 2014, they held a rally at [Fobbing](/wiki/Fobbing "Fobbing") in [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), the site of the 1381 [Peasants' Revolt](/wiki/Peasants%27_Revolt "Peasants' Revolt"), and declared, "let the English revolt begin."[[42]](#cite_note-42) The party fielded a full slate of candidates across the nine [English European parliament constituencies](/wiki/Regions_of_England "Regions of England") on 22 May 2014. On 13 February 2014, party chairman Robin Tilbrook appeared on the BBC *Daily Politics* with [Nicola Sturgeon](/wiki/Nicola_Sturgeon "Nicola Sturgeon"), the deputy leader of the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party").[[43]](#cite_note-43) The English Democrats performed poorly at the election, taking just 0.8% of the votes, down more than 1% on 2009. They again failed to win any seats.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

#### 2019

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2019")]

In May 2019, the English Democrats took part in the [elections to the European Parliament](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2019 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), fielding candidates in only four out of nine English [constituencies](/wiki/European_Parliament_constituencies_in_the_United_Kingdom "European Parliament constituencies in the United Kingdom"): [East of England](/wiki/East_of_England_(European_Parliament_constituency) "East of England (European Parliament constituency)"), [North West England](/wiki/North_West_England_(European_Parliament_constituency) "North West England (European Parliament constituency)"), [South West England](/wiki/South_West_England_(European_Parliament_constituency) "South West England (European Parliament constituency)"), and [Yorkshire and the Humber](/wiki/Yorkshire_and_the_Humber_(European_Parliament_constituency) "Yorkshire and the Humber (European Parliament constituency)"). In total they received [39,938 votes, 0.2% of the 17,199,701 valid votes cast](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom#Results "2019 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), being around 10,000 in each of the constituencies they contested. They again failed to win any seats.

### Local councils

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Local councils")]

In November 2005, the party achieved its first electoral success when Paul Adams was elected to [Crowborough](/wiki/Crowborough "Crowborough") Town Council, polling 120 votes, or 56.8% of the poll, on a turnout of 10%.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In the [2007 local elections](/wiki/2007_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2007 United Kingdom local elections"), 78 candidates stood for election in boroughs and districts in 15 English counties, including 20 in [Dartford](/wiki/2007_Dartford_Council_election "2007 Dartford Council election") and ten in [Portsmouth](/wiki/2007_Portsmouth_Council_election "2007 Portsmouth Council election"). All were unsuccessful.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2008, the party fielded candidates in 12 district council elections. None was elected. The party's best results were when it came second to the Conservatives: in the [Finningley](/wiki/Finningley "Finningley") ward of Doncaster and in three wards in Rochford.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

In the 2009 English local elections, the party fielded 84 county council and local authority candidates, with a particular focus in Bristol, Essex, Hampshire, Kent, Northamptonshire, Nottinghamshire, Wiltshire and Lancashire.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The party had an unexpected success when Peter Davies, its candidate for Mayor of Doncaster, was elected. Having received 16,961 votes in the first round, 189 votes behind the [independent](/wiki/Independent_(politician) "Independent (politician)") Michael Maye, Davies was returned in the second count on transfers of second preference votes, with 25,344 votes to 24,990.[[13]](#cite_note-DoncasterMayor2009-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Mayoral_Election_2009_Results-14)

In 2010, the party also stood in the local elections on 6 May, but did not publish a list of candidates.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The two sitting English Democrat councillors on Calderdale and Blackburn with Darwen councils retired, and the party did not nominate any candidates to contest the seats.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

In 2011, the EDP stood about 130 candidates in district level elections; two were elected. The [Boston Borough Council](/wiki/Boston_(borough) "Boston (borough)") elections on 5 May 2011, saw the election of the first two EDP councillors. David Owens and Elliott Fountain were elected in the Fenside Ward for a four-year term, thus becoming the only EDP members of a district council to be elected by public vote. A sitting EDP councillor on Peterborough council lost his seat.

The party fielded a candidate in the 3 March 2011 local by-election for the [Walkden](/wiki/Walkden "Walkden") North ward of [Salford City Council](/wiki/Salford_City_Council "Salford City Council"). Its candidate, Laurence Depares, polled 125 votes (7%) and came third, ahead of the British National Party and [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)");[[48]](#cite_note-48) in a by-election in the same city's Swinton South ward on 7 January 2014 the party was fifth with 54 votes (3.7%).[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In 2012, the party fielded 101 candidates in the local elections in England, including district council elections, mayoral contests and elections to the Greater London Assembly.[[50]](#cite_note-50) None was elected, and the party suffered the loss of the two seats it was defending, one that it had gained from a former BNP member who had defected to the party and another from an ex-Tory.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The English Democrats has come under fire from the anti-fascist groups [Unite Against Fascism](/wiki/Unite_Against_Fascism "Unite Against Fascism")[[52]](#cite_note-52) and [Hope not Hate](/wiki/Hope_not_Hate "Hope not Hate")[[53]](#cite_note-53) and from the trades unions [NASUWT](/wiki/NASUWT "NASUWT") and [Unite the Union](/wiki/Unite_the_Union "Unite the Union")[[54]](#cite_note-54) over the number of former BNP members standing for election for the party. 43% of English Democrats candidates in the 2012 local elections were former BNP members.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The EDP contested the [mayoral elections](/wiki/Directly_elected_mayors_in_England_and_Wales "Directly elected mayors in England and Wales") in [Liverpool](/wiki/Directly_elected_mayor_of_Liverpool "Directly elected mayor of Liverpool") and [Salford](/wiki/Directly_elected_mayor_of_Salford "Directly elected mayor of Salford"). In Liverpool, its candidate received 1.42% of the vote, finishing in ninth place, and in Salford 3.6% finishing in eighth place.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The party chairman, Robin Tilbrook, declared that he was standing for [Mayor of London](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London") and asked for donations through one of the party's websites but his name did not appear on the ballot paper.

### Welsh Assembly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Welsh Assembly")]

In 2007, in line with [the English Democrats stance on the status of Monmouthshire](/wiki/English_Democrats_Party#Traditional_counties "English Democrats Party"), 13 English Democrat candidates contested the [Welsh Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_for_Wales "National Assembly for Wales") elections in the South East Wales region, and the constituencies of Monmouth (fifth with 2.7%), Newport East (sixth, 2.2%) and Newport West (fifth, 2.7%).[[57]](#cite_note-57) The party also received 0.9% of the vote on the regional list.

The party contested the [2016 Welsh Assembly election](/wiki/2016_National_Assembly_for_Wales_election "2016 National Assembly for Wales election") in Monmouth. Its candidate, Stephen Morris, received 146 votes, 0.5% of the votes cast.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### London mayoral elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: London mayoral elections")]

In July 2007, Garry Bushell was nominated as the English Democrats' mayoral candidate for the [2008 London mayoral and Assembly elections](/wiki/2008_London_mayoral_election "2008 London mayoral election") with the campaign slogan "Serious About London".[[59]](#cite_note-59) In January 2008, he stepped down as candidate because of work commitments and [Matt O'Connor](/wiki/Matt_O%27Connor_(activist) "Matt O'Connor (activist)"), the founder of [Fathers 4 Justice](/wiki/Fathers_4_Justice "Fathers 4 Justice"), was selected by the English Democrats in his place with his campaign expected to start on 14 February. His campaign web site was launched on 31 January 2008.[[60]](#cite_note-60) A [party political broadcast](/wiki/Party_political_broadcast "Party political broadcast") for O'Connor's campaign was broadcast on 11 April.

One week before the election, on 25 April, O'Connor told [Vanessa Feltz](/wiki/Vanessa_Feltz "Vanessa Feltz") and the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") that he was dropping out of the Mayoral race, giving as his reasons the lack of support within the English Democrats on [St George's Day](/wiki/St_George%27s_Day "St George's Day") and a lack of press coverage[[61]](#cite_note-61) as well as the party's co-operation with the far-right group [England First](/wiki/England_First_Party "England First Party").[[62]](#cite_note-62) The English Democrats released a press statement on their website in response to his resignation voicing disappointment at his decision to quit the contest.[[63]](#cite_note-63) O'Connor received 10,695 first preference votes (representing 0.44% of the votes cast) in the mayoral contest, ranking ninth out of ten candidates; he received 73,538 second preference votes (3.67%), ranking eighth.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

In December 2015, [Winston McKenzie](/wiki/Winston_McKenzie "Winston McKenzie") announced that he had joined the English Democrats, along with confirmation that he had been selected as a London Mayoral Candidate for the 2016 London Elections.[[65]](#cite_note-65) His nomination was deemed invalid and he did not contest the mayoral election.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

## Members holding local government seats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Members holding local government seats")]

In November 2005, Paul Adams became the first elected English Democrats councillor at parish level for [Crowborough](/wiki/Crowborough "Crowborough") Town Council in [East Sussex](/wiki/East_Sussex "East Sussex"), polling 120 votes of 211 cast; he was returned in 2007 in an uncontested election. In 2011, he was elected as an independent, so the English Democrats lost the seat.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Later that year, Markyate Parish councillor Simon Deacon defected from the [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party"), to the English Democrats, having been elected unopposed. However, he resigned in October 2012.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The English Democrats gained another parish council seat when Mick Glynn was elected for the Dunsville ward of Hatfield Town Council, Doncaster. Following the resignation of Peter Davies, Glynn resigned his seat and membership of the party in February 2013.

The party had two district councillors elected (the first above parish level) in the 2011 local elections and obtained a further county council seat through the defection of a BNP, later "Independent Nationalist", councillor in Hertfordshire. However, the county councillor did not defend the seat in the [May 2013](/wiki/2013_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2013 United Kingdom local elections") election, nor did the EDP put forward another candidate, losing them the seat.[[73]](#cite_note-73)
The EDP lost one of its district councillors, Elliott Fountain, on 25 July 2013 after he failed to attend any meetings in six months.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Following the [2015 local elections](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2015 United Kingdom local elections"), the English Democrats have no representation on any local authorities.

## Electoral fraud

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Electoral fraud")]

In March 2017, Steven Uncles, the former regional chairman and candidate for the [Kent Police and Crime Commissioner](/wiki/Kent_Police_and_Crime_Commissioner "Kent Police and Crime Commissioner") election, was imprisoned for seven months after he had completed County Council nomination forms using fake names such as "Anna Cleves" and "Rachelle Stevens", or real people who had not signed the relevant forms.[[75]](#cite_note-75) He was subsequently expelled from the party for bringing it into disrepute.

## Party policies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Party policies")]

It presents itself as an English equivalent to the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP),[[76]](#cite_note-76) although the SNP is generally considered to be a centre-left party[[77]](#cite_note-77) whereas the English Democrats are on the right of the political spectrum. The English Democrats have accepted defectors from the far-right [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party") (BNP) into leadership roles and some former members of the party have criticised informal links with other [far-right](/wiki/Far-right_politics "Far-right politics") organisations, although party leader [Robin Tilbrook](/wiki/Robin_Tilbrook "Robin Tilbrook") has stated that party members are expected to pledge their opposition to [racism](/wiki/Racism "Racism").[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[15]](#cite_note-resignation-15)

### An English Parliament, but no longer independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: An English Parliament, but no longer independence")]

The English Democrat leader frequently changes the party's constitutional offering. Since 2016, they propose creating a unified, devolved English parliament, within a federal UK, not an independent sovereign state. An English executive and first minister with the same powers as Scottish ones, and a reformed second chamber at Westminster. It proposes fiscal devolution so that the English, Scottish, Welsh and Northern Irish parliaments become responsible for financing their own expenditure.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

In 2006, the party rejected suggestions that non-English MPs in the House of Commons should be barred from voting on England-specific matters, on the basis that this would lead to there being, in effect, two parliaments in the same building and that this would be problematic.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

From March 2014 to the 2015 general election, the party chairman, Robin Tilbrook, had suggested [England should become an independent country](/wiki/English_independence "English independence").[[81]](#cite_note-81) Temporarily rekindling its roots, his 2015 general election campaign launch was moved on from Traitors Gate (Tower of London), to the nearby *Hung, Drawn and Quartered* pub. Tilbrook accused various political parties of being traitors to the English (Labour, the Scottish National Party, Plaid Cymru and Sinn Féin), and the United Kingdom Independence Party "have no interest in England whatsoever". He said the party was "consolidating our position as the English nationalist party".[[82]](#cite_note-82)

### Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Immigration")]

In the party's [2019 general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election") manifesto, alongside campaigning for an independent England with an English parliament and English prime minister, the party also stated that immigration would come under the control of an English parliament. The party called for a points-style system based upon the Canadian and Australian models, and called for a withdrawal from the [1951 Refugee Convention](/wiki/Convention_Relating_to_the_Status_of_Refugees "Convention Relating to the Status of Refugees").[[83]](#cite_note-83)

### Traditional counties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Traditional counties")]

The party is supportive of [historic counties](/wiki/Counties_of_the_United_Kingdom "Counties of the United Kingdom"). It has called for a referendum on whether [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(historic) "Monmouthshire (historic)") should be part of England rather than Wales.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

It contested the [constituency of Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Monmouth (UK Parliament constituency)") in the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"), receiving 100 votes (0.2% of votes cast).[[85]](#cite_note-85)

## Nationalist connections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Nationalist connections")]

On 17 November 2011, the chairman of the English Democrats, Robin Tilbrook, met Sergey Yerzunov, a member of the executive committee of the Russian right-wing group [Russky Obraz](/w/index.php?title=Russky_Obraz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Russky Obraz (page does not exist)").[[86]](#cite_note-86)[*[independent source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Independent_sources "Wikipedia:Independent sources")*] Shortly afterwards, Obraz announced that it was in alliance with the English Democrats. Other members of this alliance include the Serbian [Obraz](/wiki/Obraz_(organization) "Obraz (organization)"), [1389 Movement](/wiki/1389_Movement "1389 Movement"), [Golden Dawn](/wiki/Golden_Dawn_(Greece) "Golden Dawn (Greece)"), [Danes' Party](/wiki/Party_of_the_Danes "Party of the Danes"), [Slovenska Pospolitost](/wiki/Slovenska_Pospolitost "Slovenska Pospolitost"), [Workers' Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(Czech_Republic) "Workers' Party (Czech Republic)") and [Noua Dreaptă](/wiki/Noua_Dreapt%C4%83 "Noua Dreaptă").[[87]](#cite_note-87)[*[independent source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Independent_sources "Wikipedia:Independent sources")*]

According to the advocacy group [Hope not Hate](/wiki/Hope_not_Hate "Hope not Hate"), a number of former members of the BNP joined the party in 2011.[[88]](#cite_note-HNH-88) Tilbrook was quoted as saying, of ex-BNP supporters, "They will help us become an electorally credible party. We need to be sure they ascribe to civic or cultural nationalism and that they will be an asset to our party, but we do not need to be too defensive."[[88]](#cite_note-HNH-88) In an April 2013 interview, Tilbrook said that about 200–300 of the party's membership of 3,000 were former BNP members. He said it was "perfectly fair" that such people would "change their minds" and join a "moderate, sensible English nationalist party".[[89]](#cite_note-89)

### New England Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: New England Party")]

The New England Party was a local party in [Dartford](/wiki/Dartford "Dartford") from March 2003 to February 2007, when it merged with the English Democrats.[[90]](#cite_note-90) Its leader, nominating officer and treasurer was Michael Tibby while Sheila Tibby was its campaigns officer.

Michael Tibby and Austen Brooker were councillors on [Dartford](/wiki/Dartford_(borough) "Dartford (borough)") Borough Council between 2003 and 2007, representing [Littlebrook](/w/index.php?title=Littlebrook&action=edit&redlink=1 "Littlebrook (page does not exist)") and [Newtown](/wiki/Newtown,_Kent "Newtown, Kent"). Tibby came second in Littlebrook in the 2003 local elections, while Brooker was elected as one of three [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") councillors for Newtown, but left Labour soon after his election in May 2003, initially as an independent.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Brooker stood down at the 2007 local elections and Tibby was unsuccessful in seeking re-election for the English Democrats, coming third.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

Michael Tibby contested [Dartford](/wiki/Dartford_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Dartford (UK Parliament constituency)") at the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election"), receiving 1,224 votes (the elected Labour [MP](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament "Member of Parliament"), [Howard Stoate](/wiki/Howard_Stoate "Howard Stoate"), received 19,909 votes). The party contested the Dartford Borough Council Heath ward by-election on 27 July 2006, polling 174 votes (9.6%). The candidate was Steven Uncles, who had been the English Democrats candidate in the [2006 Bromley and Chislehurst by-election](/wiki/2006_Bromley_and_Chislehurst_by-election "2006 Bromley and Chislehurst by-election").

The New England Party merged with the English Democrats in February 2007.[[93]](#cite_note-hnh-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Reform UK Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Democrats&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Reform UK Party")]

Not to be confused with [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK").

Reform UK Party[[95]](#cite_note-95) was a small UKIP splinter group of those opposing [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage"),[[93]](#cite_note-hnh-93)[[96]](#cite_note-96) led by Harold Green from 2000 to 2004, when it merged with the English Democrats.[[97]](#cite_note-97) Green received 357 votes (0.9%) in [Reigate](/wiki/Reigate_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Reigate (UK Parliament constituency)") in the [2001 general election](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election") standing for Reform UK Party.[[98]](#cite_note-98)
